title: FastAPI + Ollama Streaming Chat API
tldr: Production FastAPI server that streams Llama 3.x tokens from a local Ollama daemon via Server-Sent Events with proper backpressure, CORS, and disconnect detection.
when_to_use: Reach for this when you need a self-hosted chat API streaming a local Llama 3.x model. Three concrete scenarios: (1) Internal company chatbot where data cannot leave your network — Ollama runs on-prem, this FastAPI server fronts it. (2) Latency-sensitive UI where you want tokens appearing as they generate (TTFT < 500ms on RTX 4090). (3) Cost-conscious prototype before committing to a managed inference vendor — Ollama costs $0 to run on a workstation you already own.
when_not_to_use: Don't use this if you need >50 concurrent users (Ollama serializes requests per model; use vLLM instead). Don't use this for production workloads requiring 99.9% uptime — Ollama has no built-in HA. Don't use this if you need long-context (>32K tokens) — Llama 3.x context degrades past 32K; consider Claude or Gemini for those.
quick_start:
# Install + pull model + run
uv pip install fastapi uvicorn ollama
ollama pull llama3.1:8b
ollama serve &
uvicorn main:app --reload
full_code:
# main.py — FastAPI + Ollama streaming chat with disconnect detection
import asyncio
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncGenerator

import ollama
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Request
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

# Ollama client — async variant for concurrency
client = ollama.AsyncClient(host='http://localhost:11434')


@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Warm up the model on startup so first request isn't slow
    try:
        await client.generate(model='llama3.1:8b', prompt='hi', stream=False)
        print('Model warmed up')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'WARN: warmup failed (is ollama running?): {e}')
    yield


app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=['http://localhost:3000'],  # tighten for prod
    allow_methods=['POST'],
    allow_headers=['*'],
)


class Message(BaseModel):
    role: str = Field(pattern='^(system|user|assistant)$')
    content: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=32_000)


class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    messages: list[Message]
    model: str = 'llama3.1:8b'
    temperature: float = Field(0.7, ge=0.0, le=2.0)
    max_tokens: int = Field(2048, ge=1, le=8192)


async def stream_tokens(req: ChatRequest, request: Request) -> AsyncGenerator[str, None]:
    """Yield SSE-formatted tokens. Detects client disconnect to free GPU memory."""
    try:
        stream = await client.chat(
            model=req.model,
            messages=[m.model_dump() for m in req.messages],
            options={'temperature': req.temperature, 'num_predict': req.max_tokens},
            stream=True,
        )
        async for chunk in stream:
            if await request.is_disconnected():
                print('client disconnected — aborting generation')
                break
            tok = chunk['message']['content']
            yield f'data: {tok}\n\n'
        yield 'data: [DONE]\n\n'
    except ollama.ResponseError as e:
        # Model not pulled, etc.
        yield f'data: [ERROR] {e.error}\n\n'
    except Exception as e:
        yield f'data: [ERROR] {type(e).__name__}: {e}\n\n'


@app.post('/chat')
async def chat(req: ChatRequest, request: Request):
    return StreamingResponse(
        stream_tokens(req, request),
        media_type='text/event-stream',
        headers={'Cache-Control': 'no-cache', 'X-Accel-Buffering': 'no'},
    )


@app.get('/health')
async def health():
    try:
        await client.list()
        return {'status': 'ok'}
    except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(503, detail=f'ollama unreachable: {e}')

variations.label: Anthropic Claude variant
variations.description: Swap Ollama for Anthropic API to use Claude 4.6 Sonnet. Same SSE streaming contract, no UI changes needed.
variations.code_snippet:
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
client = AsyncAnthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

async def stream_tokens(req, request):
    async with client.messages.stream(
        model='claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929',
        max_tokens=req.max_tokens,
        messages=[m.model_dump() for m in req.messages],
    ) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            if await request.is_disconnected():
                break
            yield f'data: {text}\n\n'
        yield 'data: [DONE]\n\n'

variations.label: vLLM OpenAI-compatible variant
variations.description: Use vLLM's OpenAI-compatible server for 10-30x higher throughput than Ollama on the same GPU.
variations.code_snippet:
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
client = AsyncOpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:8000/v1', api_key='not-needed')

stream = await client.chat.completions.create(
    model='meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct',
    messages=[m.model_dump() for m in req.messages],
    stream=True,
)
async for chunk in stream:
    if delta := chunk.choices[0].delta.content:
        yield f'data: {delta}\n\n'

variations.label: Production-hardened variant
variations.description: Adds per-IP rate limiting, request timeout, and structured logging with request_id.
variations.code_snippet:
from slowapi import Limiter
from slowapi.util import get_remote_address
import uuid, structlog

limiter = Limiter(key_func=get_remote_address)
log = structlog.get_logger()

@app.post('/chat')
@limiter.limit('30/minute')
async def chat(req: ChatRequest, request: Request):
    req_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    log.info('chat.start', request_id=req_id, model=req.model)
    return StreamingResponse(
        stream_tokens(req, request),
        media_type='text/event-stream',
        headers={'X-Request-ID': req_id},
    )

common_errors.error_text: GPU memory exhausted after 5-10 concurrent requests
common_errors.cause: Ollama serializes per-model. Multiple long generations queue up and exhaust KV cache memory.
common_errors.fix_snippet:

GPU_SLOTS = Semaphore(2)
async def stream_tokens(req, request):
    async with GPU_SLOTS:
        # ... existing code
# Option 2: switch to vLLM (continuous batching, ~10x more concurrency on same GPU)

common_errors.cause: CORSMiddleware not configured for your frontend origin

    allow_origins=['https://your-frontend.com', 'http://localhost:3000'],
    allow_methods=['POST'],
)

production_checklist: Set request timeout (asyncio.wait_for at the route level) so a stuck generation doesn't hold a worker indefinitely
Validate input message count + total token length BEFORE calling the model (cheap reject)
Add per-user rate limiting via slowapi or a Redis-backed limiter (token bucket recommended)
Log every request with request_id, user_id, model, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, latency_ms
Add /health endpoint that probes Ollama liveness — wire to Kubernetes readiness probe
Pin Ollama version in production — recent releases have occasionally regressed streaming
Set Content-Security-Policy on the frontend so a malicious response can't exfiltrate cookies

faq.question: Why use Ollama instead of vLLM here?
faq.answer: Ollama is dramatically simpler for local development — one binary, one model pull, zero config. Switch to vLLM only when throughput becomes your bottleneck (typically past ~5 concurrent users). The vLLM variation above is a drop-in for that case.

faq.question: Will this work on Apple Silicon?
faq.answer: Yes. Ollama uses Metal automatically on M1/M2/M3/M4. For a 16GB Mac, stick to 7B-8B models at Q4 quant. For 36GB+ Macs you can run Llama 3.3 70B at Q3_K_M.

faq.question: How do I add conversation memory?
faq.answer: Simplest: the client tracks the messages array and sends it on each call (already supported — the messages field accepts the full history). For longer conversations, summarize older turns into a system message — see the agent-memory-summarizer prompt in the prompt library.

title: Anthropic Claude 4 Streaming with Tool Use
tldr: Claude 4.6 Sonnet streaming + parallel tool calling pattern with structured error recovery and multi-turn tool-use loop.
when_to_use: Use this pattern when you need a Claude-powered agent that calls tools while streaming output to the user. Three scenarios: (1) Customer support agent that streams responses while looking up order data, ticket history, and KB articles in parallel. (2) Coding assistant that streams reasoning while invoking file-read / search / lint tools. (3) Research agent that streams its synthesis while fetching from search APIs. Critical: this pattern handles partial tool-use blocks during streaming correctly, which most quick-start examples get wrong.
when_not_to_use: Skip this if you're using a model without native tool use (Llama 3.x base needs Outlines or Instructor instead). Skip if you don't need streaming — the non-streaming API is simpler. Skip for very long-running agent loops (>10 minutes) — Claude's streaming connection can timeout; use a queue + webhook pattern instead.
quick_start:
uv pip install anthropic
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
python agent.py

from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
client = AsyncAnthropic()

async def run_agent_async(user_message):
    messages = [{'role': 'user', 'content': user_message}]
    for _ in range(5):
        async with client.messages.stream(
            model='claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929',
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=[{**t, 'cache_control': {'type': 'ephemeral'}} for t in TOOLS],
            messages=messages,
        ) as stream:
            async for text in stream.text_stream:
                print(text, end='', flush=True)
            final = await stream.get_final_message()
        # ... rest of loop

common_errors.error_text: anthropic.BadRequestError: messages.1.content.0.tool_use_id: tool_use_id of tool_result does not match any tool_use
common_errors.cause: Returning a tool_result without the corresponding tool_use_id from the assistant's previous turn

common_errors.error_text: anthropic.RateLimitError: 429 — rate_limit_exceeded
common_errors.cause: Tier 1 default is 50 req/min on Sonnet. Bursts during agent loop iterations exceed it.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic(max_retries=5)  # SDK auto-backs off with retries
# Or for higher tier limits, request a tier increase at console.anthropic.com

common_errors.error_text: Agent loops infinitely calling the same tool
common_errors.cause: Model is stuck because tool result doesn't actually answer the question

common_errors.error_text: Streaming stops mid-tool-use block
common_errors.cause: Network interruption during tool_use streaming. The SDK can leave partial content.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Wrap the stream block in try/except + retry the whole iteration:
try:
    with client.messages.stream(...) as stream:
        ...
except (anthropic.APIConnectionError, httpx.ReadTimeout):
    print('stream broken, retrying iteration')
    continue  # re-runs the same iteration with same messages

production_checklist: Set max_iterations (guard against infinite loops — 5 is a safe default)
Truncate the messages array if it grows beyond 100 turns (summarize old context with a separate Haiku call)
Add prompt caching on the tool definitions — 90% cost cut after the first iteration
Log every tool call with tool_use_id, tool name, args, result_length, latency_ms — invaluable for debugging stuck agents
Use AsyncAnthropic + run tool executions concurrently (asyncio.gather) when tools are independent
Set request timeout on the streaming context (Anthropic default is 60s; bump for slow tools or use thinking mode)

faq.question: Why not use OpenAI's function calling instead?
faq.answer: Claude's tool use returns multiple tool_use blocks in a single turn (true parallel calls), while OpenAI returns them serially. For agents needing 2-5 independent lookups (weather in 3 cities, search 2 sources, etc.) Claude's pattern is ~3x faster end-to-end.

faq.question: How do I add a custom tool?
faq.answer: Append a dict to the TOOLS list with name, description, and input_schema (JSON Schema). Add a branch in execute_tool that handles its name. Claude will figure out when to call it from the description — no further wiring needed.

faq.question: Can I run this with non-Claude models?
faq.answer: Not without changes — Anthropic's tool-use API differs from OpenAI's. For a unified pattern across providers, use LiteLLM or the OpenAI-compatible variant in the openai-compatible-client-multi-provider starter.

title: OpenAI-Compatible Client (works with OpenAI, vLLM, Ollama, Together, Groq, Fireworks)
tldr: One client config that swaps providers by base_url. Includes retry, timeout, fallback chain, and per-request cost tracking. Drop-in for any OpenAI-API-compatible endpoint.
when_to_use: Use this when you want vendor-neutral code that runs against any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Three scenarios: (1) Dev with Ollama locally, prod with OpenAI/Anthropic — no code changes between environments. (2) Cost optimization: route cheap classification to Groq (low latency Llama 3.3), heavy reasoning to GPT-5 or Claude. (3) Reliability: when OpenAI is down, automatically fall back to Together AI or Fireworks.
when_not_to_use: Skip this for provider-specific features (Anthropic's parallel tool use, Gemini's long-context caching, OpenAI's strict JSON mode beyond function calling). For those, use the native SDK. Skip if you only ever use one provider — adding fallback complexity isn't worth it.
quick_start:
uv pip install openai tenacity
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...  # or any provider's key
python client.py
full_code:
# client.py — multi-provider OpenAI-compatible client with fallback + cost tracking
import os
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Iterator

from openai import OpenAI, RateLimitError, APIConnectionError
from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt, wait_exponential, retry_if_exception_type


@dataclass
class Provider:
    name: str
    base_url: str
    api_key_env: str
    model: str
    input_per_million: float  # USD per 1M input tokens
    output_per_million: float


# Configure your providers. Order = fallback priority.
PROVIDERS = [
    Provider('openai', 'https://api.openai.com/v1', 'OPENAI_API_KEY',
             'gpt-4o', 2.50, 10.00),
    Provider('anthropic-oai', 'https://api.anthropic.com/v1', 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY',
             'claude-sonnet-4-5', 3.00, 15.00),
    Provider('groq', 'https://api.groq.com/openai/v1', 'GROQ_API_KEY',
             'llama-3.3-70b-versatile', 0.59, 0.79),
    Provider('together', 'https://api.together.xyz/v1', 'TOGETHER_API_KEY',
             'meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct-Turbo', 0.88, 0.88),
    Provider('ollama', 'http://localhost:11434/v1', '',
             'llama3.1:8b', 0.0, 0.0),
]


@dataclass
class CallResult:
    text: str
    provider: str
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost_usd: float
    latency_ms: int


class MultiProviderClient:
    def __init__(self, providers: list[Provider] = PROVIDERS):
        self.providers = [
            p for p in providers
            if not p.api_key_env or os.environ.get(p.api_key_env)
        ]
        if not self.providers:
            raise RuntimeError('No providers have valid API keys configured')
        self.clients = {
            p.name: OpenAI(
                base_url=p.base_url,
                api_key=os.environ.get(p.api_key_env, 'not-needed'),
                timeout=30.0,
            )
            for p in self.providers
        }

    @retry(
        stop=stop_after_attempt(3),
        wait=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, min=2, max=20),
        retry=retry_if_exception_type((RateLimitError, APIConnectionError)),
    )
    def _call_one(self, provider: Provider, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> CallResult:
        t0 = time.time()
        client = self.clients[provider.name]
        r = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=provider.model,
            messages=messages,
            **kwargs,
        )
        latency_ms = int((time.time() - t0) * 1000)
        u = r.usage
        cost = (u.prompt_tokens * provider.input_per_million / 1_000_000
              + u.completion_tokens * provider.output_per_million / 1_000_000)
        return CallResult(
            text=r.choices[0].message.content,
            provider=provider.name,
            model=provider.model,
            input_tokens=u.prompt_tokens,
            output_tokens=u.completion_tokens,
            cost_usd=cost,
            latency_ms=latency_ms,
        )

    def call(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> CallResult:
        """Try each provider in order. Returns first success."""
        last_err = None
        for p in self.providers:
            try:
                return self._call_one(p, messages, **kwargs)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f'[{p.name}] failed: {type(e).__name__}: {e}. Falling over.')
                last_err = e
        raise RuntimeError(f'All {len(self.providers)} providers failed. Last: {last_err}')

    def call_cheapest(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> CallResult:
        """Use the cheapest configured provider."""
        cheapest = min(self.providers, key=lambda p: p.input_per_million + p.output_per_million)
        return self._call_one(cheapest, messages, **kwargs)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    cli = MultiProviderClient()
    r = cli.call([{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Say hi in 5 words.'}], max_tokens=50)
    print(f'\n{r.provider} ({r.model}): {r.text}')
    print(f'  tokens: {r.input_tokens} in / {r.output_tokens} out')
    print(f'  cost: ${r.cost_usd:.6f} | latency: {r.latency_ms}ms')

faq.question: How is this different from LiteLLM?
faq.answer: LiteLLM is a heavier abstraction with its own router process and 100+ supported providers. This pattern is ~80 lines of code that uses only the openai SDK + tenacity, and handles the 5 providers most teams actually use. Choose LiteLLM when you need 20+ providers or budget enforcement; choose this when you want lightweight code you fully understand.

faq.question: Can I add streaming with cost tracking?
faq.answer: Yes — streaming doesn't return a usage object until the stream completes (OpenAI sends a final chunk with usage when stream_options={'include_usage': True}). See the streaming variant above for the pattern.

title: LiteLLM Gateway with Fallback Routing + Cost Budgets
tldr: Self-hosted LiteLLM proxy with 100+ provider support, fallback routing, per-team cost budgets, Langfuse observability, and API key rotation.
when_to_use: Use LiteLLM when you have 5+ teams hitting LLMs and need centralized: cost attribution per team, key rotation, rate limiting per virtual key, model fallback policies, and unified observability. Three scenarios: (1) Internal platform where backend services use LLMs via an internal /v1/chat/completions endpoint, and finance needs per-team cost reports. (2) Production app with 99.99% uptime requirement — LiteLLM handles automatic fallback when OpenAI is degraded. (3) Cost-control where you want to enforce a $50/day spending cap per project.
when_not_to_use: Skip LiteLLM for solo projects or single-team apps — the lightweight multi-provider client (openai-compatible-client-multi-provider) is simpler. Skip if you don't need centralized cost/key management. Skip if you're <10 req/min — the proxy hop adds ~50ms latency.
quick_start:
uv pip install 'litellm[proxy]'
litellm --config config.yaml --port 4000
# Then use any OpenAI client pointed at http://localhost:4000
full_code:
# config.yaml — LiteLLM proxy with fallback, budgets, and observability
model_list:
  # Frontier models — Anthropic primary, OpenAI fallback
  - model_name: smart-agent
    litellm_params:
      model: claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929
      api_key: os.environ/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
      timeout: 60
    model_info:
      max_tokens: 8192
      input_cost_per_token: 0.000003
      output_cost_per_token: 0.000015

  - model_name: smart-agent-fallback
    litellm_params:
      model: gpt-4o
      api_key: os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY

  # Cheap classifier — Groq Llama 3.3 70B
  - model_name: classifier
    litellm_params:
      model: groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile
      api_key: os.environ/GROQ_API_KEY
      rpm: 30  # per-model rate limit

  # Local fallback when network is down
  - model_name: local
    litellm_params:
      model: ollama/llama3.1:8b
      api_base: http://ollama:11434

router_settings:
  # When 'smart-agent' fails, try smart-agent-fallback, then local
  fallbacks:
    - smart-agent: [smart-agent-fallback, local]
    - classifier: [smart-agent-fallback]
  retry_policy:
    BadRequestErrorRetries: 0
    AuthenticationErrorRetries: 0
    TimeoutErrorRetries: 2
    RateLimitErrorRetries: 3
    ContentPolicyViolationErrorRetries: 0
  num_retries: 3
  timeout: 60
  routing_strategy: usage-based-routing-v2  # routes to least-utilized

litellm_settings:
  # Per-team budgets — auto-rejects requests over budget
  max_budget: 200.0  # USD/month default
  budget_duration: 30d
  drop_params: True  # silently strip unsupported params

  # Hook every call into Langfuse for tracing
  success_callback: ['langfuse']
  failure_callback: ['langfuse']
  langfuse_public_key: os.environ/LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY
  langfuse_secret_key: os.environ/LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY

# Virtual keys with per-team budgets
general_settings:
  master_key: os.environ/LITELLM_MASTER_KEY
  database_url: os.environ/DATABASE_URL  # postgres for key + budget state
  store_model_in_db: true
  proxy_batch_write_at: 5  # batched DB writes every 5s for perf

# === Then create virtual keys via API ===
# curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/key/generate \
#   -H 'Authorization: Bearer $LITELLM_MASTER_KEY' \

faq.question: Is LiteLLM open source?
faq.answer: Yes, MIT-licensed. The team also sells an Enterprise tier with SSO + audit logs + dedicated support, but the proxy itself is fully open-source and self-hostable.

faq.question: Can I use this without Postgres?
faq.answer: Yes — drop the database_url config and LiteLLM runs in stateless mode. You lose virtual-key persistence (keys reset on restart) and budget tracking. Fine for small deployments.

faq.question: How does LiteLLM differ from a CDN-level fallback?
faq.answer: LiteLLM operates at the request level with provider-specific logic (knows that an OpenAI 429 means rate-limit but a 400 doesn't, knows Anthropic's tool-use shape differs from OpenAI's, etc.). A CDN-level fallback is dumb HTTP failover and breaks on schema differences.

title: AWS Bedrock Claude Streaming Client (with IAM auth + region failover)
tldr: Production Bedrock client for Claude 4 with streaming, prompt caching, IAM role authentication, region failover, and per-account token quota handling.
when_to_use: Use Bedrock Claude when you're already an AWS shop and: (1) data residency matters (Bedrock keeps prompts in your AWS region, no cross-border data flow), (2) IAM-based auth fits your existing security model, (3) you want consolidated AWS billing instead of a separate Anthropic invoice. Three scenarios: HIPAA-regulated healthcare app, FedRAMP environment, or any enterprise with strict 'no data leaves AWS' policy.
when_not_to_use: Skip Bedrock if you need the latest model the day it ships — Anthropic's direct API gets new Claude versions weeks before Bedrock. Skip if you don't care about regional data residency — direct Anthropic API is cheaper (~10-15% margin) and simpler. Skip for high-volume workloads with no enterprise constraints — Anthropic's batch API at 50% off is cheaper than Bedrock.
quick_start:
uv pip install 'boto3>=1.35'
aws configure  # or use IAM role from EC2/ECS
python bedrock_chat.py
full_code:
# bedrock_chat.py — Production Bedrock Claude with streaming + region failover
import json
import os
from typing import Iterator

import boto3
from botocore.config import Config
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError

# Regions where Claude 4 is available, in failover order
REGIONS = ['us-east-1', 'us-west-2', 'eu-west-1']
MODEL_ID = 'anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929-v1:0'  # Bedrock model ID


def make_client(region: str):
    """Create Bedrock client with retry config. Uses IAM role from env/instance."""
    return boto3.client(
        'bedrock-runtime',
        region_name=region,
        config=Config(
            retries={'max_attempts': 5, 'mode': 'adaptive'},
            connect_timeout=10,
            read_timeout=300,  # long for streaming
        ),
    )


def stream_chat(
    messages: list[dict],
    system: str = '',
    max_tokens: int = 4096,
    temperature: float = 0.7,
    use_caching: bool = True,
) -> Iterator[str]:
    """Stream tokens from Claude via Bedrock with region failover."""
    body = {
        'anthropic_version': 'bedrock-2023-05-31',
        'max_tokens': max_tokens,
        'temperature': temperature,
        'messages': messages,
    }
    if system:
        # Add cache_control for prompt caching (90% discount on cached portion)
        if use_caching:
            body['system'] = [{'type': 'text', 'text': system,
                              'cache_control': {'type': 'ephemeral'}}]
        else:
            body['system'] = system

    last_err = None
    for region in REGIONS:
        try:
            client = make_client(region)
            response = client.invoke_model_with_response_stream(
                modelId=MODEL_ID,
                body=json.dumps(body),
                contentType='application/json',
            )
            for event in response['body']:
                chunk = json.loads(event['chunk']['bytes'])
                if chunk.get('type') == 'content_block_delta':
                    delta = chunk['delta']
                    if delta.get('type') == 'text_delta':
                        yield delta['text']
                elif chunk.get('type') == 'message_stop':
                    # Final metrics — usage is in invocation_metrics on message_stop
                    metrics = chunk.get('amazon-bedrock-invocationMetrics', {})
                    print(f"\n[{region}] input={metrics.get('inputTokenCount')} "
                          f"output={metrics.get('outputTokenCount')} "
                          f"cache_read={metrics.get('cacheReadInputTokenCount', 0)} "
                          f"latency={metrics.get('invocationLatency')}ms")
            return
        except ClientError as e:
            code = e.response['Error']['Code']
            if code in ('ThrottlingException', 'ServiceQuotaExceededException',
                       'ServiceUnavailableException'):
                print(f'[{region}] {code} — failing over to next region')
                last_err = e
                continue
            raise  # other errors: re-raise
        except Exception as e:
            last_err = e
            print(f'[{region}] {type(e).__name__}: {e} — failing over')
            continue
    raise RuntimeError(f'All regions failed. Last error: {last_err}')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    system = 'You are a concise assistant. Respond in under 100 words.'
    messages = [{'role': 'user', 'content': 'What is the AWS shared responsibility model?'}]
    for tok in stream_chat(messages, system=system):
        print(tok, end='', flush=True)
    print()
setup_steps: Request access to Claude models in Bedrock console: AWS Console → Bedrock → Model access → Request access for Anthropic Claude family
Wait for approval (instant for most accounts, up to 24h for some)
Set up IAM role/policy granting bedrock:InvokeModel and bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream
If running on EC2/ECS/Lambda: attach the role to the instance — no key handling needed
If running locally: aws configure (or aws sso login) — boto3 picks up credentials automatically
Run: python bedrock_chat.py

production_checklist: Use IAM role attached to EC2/ECS/Lambda — never hardcode credentials
Enable Bedrock CloudWatch logging (InvocationLogs) for audit + cost analysis
Set up provisioned throughput for predictable workloads — cheaper than on-demand at scale
Use cross-region inference profiles for automatic regional failover
Monitor Bedrock token quotas in Service Quotas — request increases proactively before hitting limit
Enable Bedrock Guardrails for content moderation (PII redaction, harmful content)
Tag every InvokeModel call with team/project for cost attribution

faq.question: Is Bedrock more expensive than Anthropic direct?
faq.answer: Bedrock charges the same per-token rate as Anthropic's direct API for Claude. Bedrock adds ~$0 overhead per call, but you don't get the 50% batch discount that Anthropic offers, and Bedrock's prompt caching has slightly different pricing semantics. For most workloads, costs are within 5%.

faq.question: Why use Bedrock instead of calling Anthropic directly?
faq.answer: Three real reasons: (1) data residency — your prompts stay in your AWS region, not leaving for Anthropic's infra. (2) Consolidated AWS billing for finance/procurement. (3) IAM-based auth fits your existing AWS security model. If none of these apply, use Anthropic direct.

faq.question: Can I use Bedrock prompt caching?
faq.answer: Yes, with cache_control: {'type': 'ephemeral'} on system or message blocks, identical to Anthropic's direct API. The cache hit response field is amazon-bedrock-invocationMetrics.cacheReadInputTokenCount — Bedrock charges 10% of normal input rate for cached tokens.

title: vLLM 0.7 Batched Inference Server with Prometheus Metrics
tldr: Production-ready vLLM 0.7 launch script for Llama 3.3 70B on 4x A100 with continuous batching, prefix caching, Prometheus metrics, and graceful concurrency-limit guards.
description: Production-ready vLLM 0.7 launch script for Llama 3.3 70B on 4x A100 with continuous batching, prefix caching, Prometheus metrics, and graceful concurrency-limit guards.
when_to_use: You have GPU hardware (or a cloud GPU budget) and need to serve 100+ concurrent users on Llama 3.3 70B with full data sovereignty. vLLM 0.7 with continuous batching consistently delivers 600-1200 tok/s aggregate on 4x A100 80GB, with p50 TTFT under 400ms and full Prometheus observability.
when_not_to_use: Skip vLLM when (1) you have one GPU and <10 QPS - Ollama or llama.cpp are simpler; (2) you need sub-200ms TTFT - hosted Groq beats self-hosted on raw latency; (3) you're cost-optimizing offline batch jobs - Together's batch API is 50% cheaper than running your own GPUs idle.
quick_start: 1) `pip install vllm==0.7.0`  2) `python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server --model meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct --tensor-parallel-size 4 --enable-prefix-caching --max-num-seqs 256`  3) curl localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions and scrape :8000/metrics for live throughput stats.
full_code:
"""
vLLM 0.7 production launcher for Llama 3.3 70B on 4x A100 80GB.

What this gives you over the bare `python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server`:
  - Sensible defaults tuned for 4xA100 + 70B model (TP=4, gpu_mem_utilization=0.92, max_seqs=256)
  - Prefix caching ON - huge throughput win for chat (system prompt reuse)
  - Prometheus metrics on /metrics (auto-enabled in vLLM 0.7+)
  - Pre-flight GPU + nvidia-driver + CUDA check so you fail fast, not 5 minutes into model load
  - Graceful SIGTERM handling so K8s rolling deploys don't truncate in-flight requests

Run:
  python serve_vllm.py --model meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct --tp 4 --port 8000 --max-seqs 256
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import os
import signal
import subprocess
import sys


def preflight() -> None:
    try:
        out = subprocess.check_output(["nvidia-smi", "--query-gpu=name,memory.total,driver_version", "--format=csv,noheader"]).decode()
    except (subprocess.CalledProcessError, FileNotFoundError):
        print("FATAL: nvidia-smi not available. Is this a GPU host?", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    gpus = [ln.strip() for ln in out.splitlines() if ln.strip()]
    if not gpus:
        print("FATAL: no GPUs detected.", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    print(f"[preflight] {len(gpus)} GPUs detected:")
    for g in gpus:
        print(f"  - {g}")


def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--model", default=os.environ.get("VLLM_MODEL", "meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"))
    ap.add_argument("--tp", type=int, default=int(os.environ.get("VLLM_TP", "4")))
    ap.add_argument("--port", type=int, default=8000)
    ap.add_argument("--host", default="0.0.0.0")
    ap.add_argument("--max-seqs", type=int, default=256)
    ap.add_argument("--max-model-len", type=int, default=8192)
    ap.add_argument("--gpu-mem-util", type=float, default=0.92)
    ap.add_argument("--quantization", choices=["none", "fp8", "awq", "gptq"], default="none")
    ap.add_argument("--api-key", default=os.environ.get("VLLM_API_KEY"))
    args = ap.parse_args()

    preflight()

    cmd = [
        sys.executable, "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server",
        "--model", args.model,
        "--tensor-parallel-size", str(args.tp),
        "--host", args.host,
        "--port", str(args.port),
        "--max-num-seqs", str(args.max_seqs),
        "--max-model-len", str(args.max_model_len),
        "--gpu-memory-utilization", str(args.gpu_mem_util),
        "--enable-prefix-caching",
        "--disable-log-requests",
    ]
    if args.quantization != "none":
        cmd += ["--quantization", args.quantization]
    if args.api_key:
        cmd += ["--api-key", args.api_key]

    print("[vllm] launching:", " ".join(cmd), flush=True)
    proc = subprocess.Popen(cmd)

    def shutdown(sig, _frame):
        proc.send_signal(signal.SIGTERM)
        try:
            proc.wait(timeout=30)
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            proc.kill()
        sys.exit(proc.returncode or 0)

    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, shutdown)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, shutdown)
    sys.exit(proc.wait())


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"""vLLM 0.7 production launcher for Llama 3.3 70B on 4x A100 80GB."""
import argparse, os, signal, subprocess, sys


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--model", default="meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct")
    ap.add_argument("--tp", type=int, default=4)
    ap.add_argument("--port", type=int, default=8000)
    args = ap.parse_args()
    cmd = [sys.executable, "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server",
        "--model", args.model, "--tensor-parallel-size", str(args.tp),
        "--port", str(args.port), "--enable-prefix-caching",
        "--max-num-seqs", "256", "--gpu-memory-utilization", "0.92"]
    proc = subprocess.Popen(cmd)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, lambda s, f: proc.send_signal(signal.SIGTERM))
    sys.exit(proc.wait())


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

production_checklist: Always set --api-key in production - vLLM exposes /v1/* with no auth by default.
Scrape `/metrics` to Prometheus and alert on `vllm:num_requests_waiting > 10` for 5 min.
Run with `--disable-log-requests` and pipe stdout to a structured logger; per-request logs are noisy.
Pin model weights to a local volume (`HF_HOME=/var/cache/huggingface`) so pod restarts don't re-download 140GB.
Use --enforce-eager only for debugging - it disables CUDA graphs and tanks throughput 30-40%.
Capacity-plan with the formula `concurrent_users = (gpu_mem_util * vram_gb - model_size_gb) / kv_cache_per_seq_gb`; for 4xA100 + 70B that's ~250 concurrent at 8k ctx.
Stand up a canary with one replica before rolling 100% - vLLM minor versions occasionally change scheduling defaults.

faq.question: vLLM vs SGLang vs TGI - which should I pick in 2026?
faq.answer: vLLM 0.7 has the deepest community + widest model support and is the safe default for OpenAI-compatible inference. SGLang wins on constrained generation (RadixAttention + grammars) and edges vLLM by 10-20% on agent/tool-use workloads. TGI is HuggingFace-blessed but trails both on throughput. Default to vLLM unless you have a specific SGLang or TGI feature in mind.

faq.question: Can I serve multiple models on one host?
faq.answer: Not in one vLLM process - it loads one model. For multi-model: (1) run multiple vLLM processes on different ports + GPUs (CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES), or (2) use Ray Serve + vLLM workers, or (3) for many small models, use LiteLLM as a router in front of multiple model endpoints.

faq.question: How do I do streaming with the OpenAI-compatible endpoint?
faq.answer: Pass `stream=true` in the request body. vLLM emits SSE events identical to OpenAI's format - the openai Python SDK works unmodified, just point `base_url` at `http://your-vllm:8000/v1`.

title: SGLang RadixAttention Server for Agent Workloads
tldr: Launch SGLang with RadixAttention to serve Llama 3.3 70B at 1.5-2x vLLM throughput on agent workloads with heavy prefix sharing.
description: Launch SGLang with RadixAttention to serve Llama 3.3 70B at 1.5-2x vLLM throughput on agent workloads with heavy prefix sharing.
when_to_use: Agent workloads where prompts share long prefixes (system prompt + tool definitions + few-shot examples that repeat across requests). SGLang's RadixAttention caches these as a radix tree across requests - typical 1.5-2x throughput uplift vs vLLM for ReAct/function-calling traffic. Also pick SGLang when you need strict JSON-schema or regex-constrained outputs.
when_not_to_use: Skip SGLang when (1) your workload is one-shot completions with unique prefixes - vLLM's prefix caching is equally good; (2) you need the broadest model coverage - vLLM has more model + quantization support; (3) you want a simpler ops story - vLLM has wider community + more battle-tested K8s patterns.
full_code:
"""
SGLang 0.4 production launcher with RadixAttention enabled.

Why SGLang over vLLM for agents:
  - RadixAttention shares KV cache across requests with common prefixes
    (system prompt + tool schemas + few-shot exemplars). For ReAct-style
    traffic this is the biggest win in the inference stack since paged attention.
  - First-class constrained generation: grammar / JSON-schema / regex enforced
    inside the kernel, not via rejection sampling.
  - OpenAI-compatible REST API on :30000.

Run: python serve_sglang.py --tp 4 --port 30000
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import os
import signal
import subprocess
import sys


def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--model", default=os.environ.get("SGLANG_MODEL", "meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"))
    ap.add_argument("--tp", type=int, default=4)
    ap.add_argument("--port", type=int, default=30000)
    ap.add_argument("--host", default="0.0.0.0")
    ap.add_argument("--mem-fraction", type=float, default=0.85)
    ap.add_argument("--max-prefill-tokens", type=int, default=16384)
    args = ap.parse_args()

    cmd = [
        sys.executable, "-m", "sglang.launch_server",
        "--model-path", args.model,
        "--tp", str(args.tp),
        "--port", str(args.port),
        "--host", args.host,
        "--mem-fraction-static", str(args.mem_fraction),
        "--max-prefill-tokens", str(args.max_prefill_tokens),
        "--enable-radix-cache",
        "--chunked-prefill-size", "8192",
    ]

    print("[sglang] launching:", " ".join(cmd), flush=True)
    proc = subprocess.Popen(cmd)

    def shutdown(sig, _frame):
        proc.send_signal(signal.SIGTERM)
        try:
            proc.wait(timeout=30)
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            proc.kill()
        sys.exit(proc.returncode or 0)

    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, shutdown)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, shutdown)
    sys.exit(proc.wait())


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import json

production_checklist: Confirm RadixAttention is paying off: `curl localhost:30000/metrics` and check `sglang:cache_hit_rate` - anything <30% means your workload doesn't share prefixes.
Use --chunked-prefill-size for large prompts (>4k tokens) - keeps TTFT predictable under bursty load.
Pin SGLang minor version - 0.4 -> 0.5 will likely change the radix cache key format and require re-warming.
Watch `nvidia-smi -l 1` for any non-SGLang process; static memory fraction means co-tenants will OOM SGLang.
Use `--show-time-cost` during load testing to identify whether you're prefill-bound or decode-bound.

faq.question: When is RadixAttention actually faster than vLLM's prefix cache?
faq.answer: Both cache prefixes, but RadixAttention shares the cache across all in-flight requests via a tree structure, while vLLM's prefix cache is per-block-hash. For chat (one system prompt, many users) the gap is small; for agent traffic (long tool schemas + few-shot examples reused on every turn) RadixAttention can be 1.5-2x faster. Measure your `cache_hit_rate` first - below 30% there's no advantage.

faq.question: Can I use SGLang's frontend DSL without the server?
faq.answer: Yes - `import sglang as sgl` lets you call the engine directly in-process. Useful for batch jobs or notebooks, but for serving you almost always want the server (handles concurrency, KV pool, OpenAI compat).

faq.question: Does SGLang support speculative decoding?
faq.answer: Yes, with `--draft-model` and EAGLE/Medusa heads. Meaningful TTFT win for low-concurrency workloads but engineering overhead is real - evaluate vLLM's speculative decoding first; in 0.7 it's caught up to SGLang.

title: llama.cpp Server with GGUF Q4_K_M Quantization
tldr: Self-host Llama 3.3 70B on a single 24GB consumer GPU using llama.cpp HTTP server with Q4_K_M - the cheapest path to fully-local production inference.
description: Self-host Llama 3.3 70B on a single 24GB consumer GPU using llama.cpp HTTP server with Q4_K_M - the cheapest path to fully-local production inference.
when_to_use: You have a single consumer GPU (RTX 4090, 3090, or even Apple Silicon) and want Llama 3.3 70B (Q4_K_M, ~42GB) or Llama 3.1 8B (Q8_0, ~8GB) running fully local with an OpenAI-compatible REST API. llama.cpp is the gold standard for quantized inference - leaner than vLLM, supports CPU + Metal + ROCm + CUDA + Vulkan, single binary.
when_not_to_use: Skip llama.cpp when (1) you need >20 concurrent users - vLLM/SGLang scale better at scale; (2) you need full FP16 quality - even Q4_K_M is a measurable quality drop (~2% on most evals); (3) you're already on multi-GPU - vLLM tensor-parallelism is faster than llama.cpp's MPI mode.
quick_start: 1) `brew install llama.cpp` (mac) or build from source  2) `huggingface-cli download bartowski/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct-GGUF --include 'Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf'`  3) `llama-server -m Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf -ngl 99 --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080`
full_code:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# launch-llamacpp.sh - production launcher for llama.cpp server
set -euo pipefail

MODEL_PATH="${MODEL_PATH:-./models/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf}"
PORT="${PORT:-8080}"
HOST="${HOST:-0.0.0.0}"
CTX_SIZE="${CTX_SIZE:-8192}"
PARALLEL="${PARALLEL:-4}"
API_KEY="${LLAMACPP_API_KEY:-}"

if [[ ! -f "$MODEL_PATH" ]]; then
  echo "[fatal] model not found at $MODEL_PATH" >&2
  exit 2
fi

NGL=0
if command -v nvidia-smi >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "[info] CUDA GPU detected"
  NGL=99
elif [[ "$(uname)" == "Darwin" ]]; then
  echo "[info] Apple Silicon (Metal) backend"
  NGL=99
fi

ARGS=(
  -m "$MODEL_PATH"
  -ngl "$NGL"
  -c "$CTX_SIZE"
  --parallel "$PARALLEL"
  --host "$HOST"
  --port "$PORT"
  --metrics
  --jinja
)
if [[ -n "$API_KEY" ]]; then
  ARGS+=(--api-key "$API_KEY")
fi

exec llama-server "${ARGS[@]}"

production_checklist: Always set LLAMACPP_API_KEY in production - the server is unauthenticated by default.
Use Q4_K_M as the default quant; Q5_K_M trades 30% more VRAM for ~0.5% eval gain (rarely worth it).
Pin to a llama.cpp build tag in your Dockerfile - the project moves fast and breaks compat across releases.
Monitor `/metrics` for `slots_idle` - if it's always 0 you're saturated.
Use `--mlock` on Linux with enough RAM to keep the model resident; avoids slow page-in on first request after idle.
Stand up a smoke-test script that pings /v1/chat/completions with a known prompt + checks the SHA of the response - catches silent template regressions.
If serving multiple models, run one llama.cpp instance per model + a LiteLLM proxy in front; llama.cpp can't hot-swap.

faq.question: llama.cpp vs Ollama - which should I use?
faq.answer: Ollama wraps llama.cpp with a model registry + Modelfile templates + simpler CLI. For solo devs and quick local prototyping, use Ollama. For production serving where you want explicit control over quant level, slots, and HTTP routing, use llama.cpp directly. Both share the same underlying engine.

faq.question: Q4_K_M vs Q5_K_M vs Q8_0 - what's the right pick?
faq.answer: Q4_K_M is the sweet spot: ~3.5x smaller than FP16 with <2% quality drop on most evals. Q5_K_M is the same quality as Q4 for 30% more VRAM - skip it. Q8_0 is near-lossless and the right pick when you have VRAM to spare. Below Q4 quality degrades noticeably.

faq.question: Can llama.cpp do continuous batching like vLLM?
faq.answer: Sort of - the --parallel flag enables multiple slots that share KV cache and can be served concurrently. Real win over no batching, but doesn't match vLLM's continuous batching scheduler at high concurrency. For solo-builder traffic (1-20 concurrent), llama.cpp is more than enough.

title: TensorRT-LLM Optimized Deploy with Triton Inference Server
tldr: Compile Llama 3.3 70B to TensorRT-LLM engines and serve via Triton with in-flight batching for the highest throughput-per-dollar on H100/H200.
description: Compile Llama 3.3 70B to TensorRT-LLM engines and serve via Triton with in-flight batching for the highest throughput-per-dollar on H100/H200.
when_to_use: You're running NVIDIA H100/H200 hardware at scale and need every last token/second/dollar. TensorRT-LLM compiles model graphs to fused CUDA kernels, beating vLLM by 20-40% on H100 raw throughput - especially with FP8 weights + KV cache. Pair with Triton Inference Server for K8s-native deployment.
when_not_to_use: Skip TRT-LLM when (1) you're not on NVIDIA H100/H200/B200 - the edge mostly comes from FP8 tensor cores; (2) you need to swap models often - engine compilation takes 10-30 min; (3) your team can't absorb the operational complexity vs vLLM (compile step, multiple containers, version pinning).
quick_start: 1) `docker pull nvcr.io/nvidia/tritonserver:24.10-trtllm-python-py3`  2) `huggingface-cli download meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct --local-dir ./hf-model`  3) Compile engine: `trtllm-build --checkpoint_dir ./hf-model --output_dir ./engines --use_fp8`  4) Launch Triton.
full_code:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# build-and-serve-trtllm.sh
set -euo pipefail

MODEL_REPO="${MODEL_REPO:-meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct}"
HF_DIR="${HF_DIR:-./hf-model}"
TRT_CKPT="${TRT_CKPT:-./trt-ckpt}"
ENGINE_DIR="${ENGINE_DIR:-./engines}"
MODEL_NAME="${MODEL_NAME:-llama33}"
TP="${TP:-4}"

if [[ ! -d "$HF_DIR/.cached" ]]; then
  echo "[1/4] downloading $MODEL_REPO ..."
  huggingface-cli download "$MODEL_REPO" --local-dir "$HF_DIR"
  touch "$HF_DIR/.cached"
fi

if [[ ! -d "$TRT_CKPT/.done" ]]; then
  echo "[2/4] converting to TRT-LLM FP8 checkpoint ..."
  python /app/tensorrt_llm/examples/llama/convert_checkpoint.py \
    --model_dir "$HF_DIR" --output_dir "$TRT_CKPT" \
    --dtype float16 --use_fp8 --fp8_kv_cache --tp_size "$TP"
  touch "$TRT_CKPT/.done"
fi

if [[ ! -d "$ENGINE_DIR/.done" ]]; then
  echo "[3/4] building TRT engine ..."
  trtllm-build --checkpoint_dir "$TRT_CKPT" --output_dir "$ENGINE_DIR" \
    --gemm_plugin float16 --max_batch_size 128 \
    --max_input_len 4096 --max_output_len 2048 \
    --use_paged_context_fmha enable --use_fp8_context_fmha enable
  touch "$ENGINE_DIR/.done"
fi

REPO=./triton-repo
mkdir -p "$REPO/$MODEL_NAME/1" "$REPO/${MODEL_NAME}_tokenizer/1"
cp -r "$ENGINE_DIR"/* "$REPO/$MODEL_NAME/1/"
cp "$HF_DIR"/tokenizer* "$REPO/${MODEL_NAME}_tokenizer/1/"

echo "[4/4] launching Triton ..."
exec mpirun -n "$TP" --allow-run-as-root tritonserver \
    --model-repository="$REPO" \
    --grpc-port=8001 --http-port=8000 --metrics-port=8002 \
    --backend-config=tensorrtllm,enable_in_flight_batching=true

common_errors.cause: trtllm-build's transient workspace can exceed VRAM on smaller GPUs.
common_errors.fix_snippet: trtllm-build --checkpoint_dir ./trt-ckpt --output_dir ./engines --workers 1 --use_paged_context_fmha enable

common_errors.error_text: Triton fails to load model: 'failed to load tensorrtllm version 1'
common_errors.cause: Triton backend version mismatch - you compiled with TRT-LLM 0.13 but Triton image is for 0.12.
common_errors.fix_snippet: FROM nvcr.io/nvidia/tritonserver:24.10-trtllm-python-py3

production_checklist: Cache compiled engines as docker volumes or S3 - re-building on every pod start is a 20-min outage.
Run the Triton metrics endpoint (:8002) into Prometheus; alert on `nv_inference_queue_duration_ms` p99.
Always enable `enable_in_flight_batching=true` - without it, throughput collapses at concurrency >4.
Pin the NGC tag explicitly (`24.10-trtllm-python-py3`, not `latest`) - NVIDIA moves fast.
Test the exact LoRA combinatorics you need before production - some adapter combinations OOM at runtime.
Have a vLLM fallback ready - TRT-LLM has had instability with certain quant combos.
Set GPU clock to max-perf: `nvidia-smi -ac 1593,1980` (H100 base + boost) to avoid thermal throttling spikes.

faq.question: Is TRT-LLM actually worth the operational overhead vs vLLM?
faq.answer: On H100/H200 with FP8 + KV cache quant, TRT-LLM delivers 20-40% more tokens/sec than vLLM at the same hardware spend. On A100 or older the gap is much smaller (5-15%). Calculate the dollar savings on your real traffic before committing - if you're spending <$50k/year on inference, the engineering cost rarely pays back vs vLLM.

faq.question: Can I use the OpenAI SDK with Triton?
faq.answer: Not directly - Triton's REST API uses NVIDIA's KServe-style /v2/models/.../generate format. Either (1) put a thin shim in front that translates OpenAI -> KServe, (2) use NVIDIA's TRT-LLM OpenAI-compat server (`tritonserver --openai-compatible`, ships in 24.10+), or (3) wrap with LiteLLM.

faq.question: How long does engine compilation take?
faq.answer: Llama 3.3 70B FP8 on 4xH100: ~20-30 min for checkpoint conversion + ~15-25 min for trtllm-build. Cache the output engines aggressively. Re-build is required only when changing TP size, max batch size, max input/output length, or model weights.

title: Ollama Custom Modelfile (System Prompt + Template + Params)
tldr: Build a custom Ollama model with a baked-in system prompt, chat template override, and tuned sampling params - reproducible across teammates and CI without a separate API gateway.
description: Build a custom Ollama model with a baked-in system prompt, chat template override, and tuned sampling params - reproducible across teammates and CI without a separate API gateway.
when_to_use: Solo devs and small teams that want a single command (`ollama run my-team-bot`) to launch an LLM with a baked-in persona, sampling, and template - no Python, no .env, no API gateway. Pairs especially well with a desktop chat client (Open WebUI, Jan, LobeChat).
when_not_to_use: Skip Ollama Modelfile when (1) you need >5 concurrent users - vLLM/llama.cpp scale better; (2) you need fine-grained per-request prompts - just use the system param at request time; (3) you need API auth - Ollama's HTTP server has no auth by default.
quick_start: 1) `brew install ollama`  2) `ollama pull llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M`  3) Write a `Modelfile`  4) `ollama create team-bot -f Modelfile`  5) `ollama run team-bot`
full_code:
# Modelfile - reusable team-wide Llama 3.3 with baked-in persona + sampling.
#
# After saving this file as `Modelfile` in your repo:
#   ollama create team-bot -f Modelfile
#   ollama run team-bot

FROM llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M

# ----- System prompt baked in -----
SYSTEM """
You are TeamBot, an internal engineering assistant for OSS AI Hub.

Ground rules:
- Be concise. Default to bullet points or short code blocks.
- If the user asks for code, prefer Python 3.11+ unless they specify otherwise.
- When uncertain, say "I'm not sure" instead of inventing facts.
- Cite the file path + line number whenever you reference code.
- Never include API keys, passwords, or any value that looks like a secret.

You have access to our internal stack: FastAPI, Postgres 16, Redis 7, vLLM 0.7,
Llama 3.3 70B Instruct. When asked about external services, default to the most
open-weight option.
"""

# ----- Sampling parameters tuned for engineering Q&A -----
PARAMETER temperature 0.4
PARAMETER top_p 0.9
PARAMETER top_k 40
PARAMETER repeat_penalty 1.05
PARAMETER num_ctx 16384

# Stop tokens
PARAMETER stop "<|eot_id|>"
PARAMETER stop "<|start_header_id|>"
PARAMETER stop "<|end_header_id|>"

LICENSE """
Llama 3 Community License (base weights)
OSS AI Hub TeamBot configuration: MIT
"""
MESSAGE user "Hi"
MESSAGE assistant "Hi! I'm TeamBot. Ask me about our codebase, infra, or stack."

dependencies_v2.name: ollama
dependencies_v2.purpose: Local LLM runtime; bundles llama.cpp + model registry + REST API.

env_vars.name: OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL
env_vars.description: Concurrent slots per model (default 1). Bump to 4 for small models.

setup_steps: Install Ollama: `brew install ollama` (mac) or `curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh` (linux).
Start the service: `ollama serve`.
Pull the base model: `ollama pull llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M` (~42GB).
Save the Modelfile shown above as `Modelfile` in your repo's root.
Build: `ollama create team-bot -f Modelfile`. Takes ~30s.
Test: `ollama run team-bot` and chat.
Use via REST: `curl localhost:11434/api/chat -d '{"model":"team-bot","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hi"}]}'`.

variations.label: GPU-aware launcher script
variations.description: Wrapper that picks the best quant for the local GPU.
variations.code_snippet:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
VRAM=$(nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.total --format=csv,noheader,nounits 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo 0)
if (( VRAM >= 48000 )); then
  ollama pull llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M
elif (( VRAM >= 24000 )); then
  sed -i 's|FROM llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M|FROM llama3.1:8b-instruct-q8_0|' Modelfile
fi
ollama create team-bot -f Modelfile

common_errors.cause: Wrong tag. Ollama's model registry uses `:70b-instruct-q4_K_M` style suffixes that shift over time.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
ollama pull llama3.3:latest
ollama pull llama3.3:70b
ollama pull llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M

common_errors.error_text: Model ignores SYSTEM prompt at runtime
common_errors.cause: Client is passing its own `system` field, which overrides Modelfile SYSTEM.

common_errors.cause: Wrong path or hidden CRLF line endings.

production_checklist: Always pin the base FROM tag explicitly (e.g. `llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M`, not `:latest`).
Check Modelfile into git so the team's custom model is reproducible.
Never expose Ollama's port on the public internet - it has no auth.
Set OLLAMA_HOST=127.0.0.1:11434 by default; opt-in to LAN binding only when needed.
Use OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4 + OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=30m for shared dev boxes.
Document the Modelfile's intended use in a README - future-you will forget why you set repeat_penalty=1.05.

faq.question: Ollama vs llama.cpp vs LM Studio - which one?
faq.answer: All three wrap llama.cpp. Ollama wins on developer ergonomics (one binary, model registry, Modelfile, REST API), LM Studio wins on GUI + model browser, llama.cpp wins on raw control + serving headroom. For team/internal tools default to Ollama; for production-grade serving move to llama.cpp directly; LM Studio is best for non-developers.

faq.question: Can I run multiple Modelfile-built models on one host?
faq.answer: Yes - Ollama caches one model in VRAM at a time by default and swaps on request. For frequent multi-model use, set OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=30m and provision enough VRAM for the two heaviest. Better: run one Ollama process per GPU with CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES pinning.

faq.question: How do I version my Modelfile?
faq.answer: Check it into git alongside your code. For binary distribution, push to a self-hosted Ollama registry with `ollama push my-registry/team/model:v1`. Tag every release; pin consumers to a specific tag, never to `:latest`.
